title: 8.8 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 8.8!

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did the exploitation of former colonies continue after their independence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Newly independent nations often remained dependent on former colonizers due to their reliance on cash crops.
There were people in the former colonies who wanted the colonizers to stay.
The newly independent nations were poor and destitute places where people couldn’t survive without outside help.
The newly independent nations’ nationalization projects failed, so they needed to maintain ties with their former colonizers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did African and Asian leaders resist dependence on their former colonizers?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They nationalized major industries to reduce foreign control.
They joined the Non-Aligned Movement to stay independent in global politics.
They isolated themselves from global trade in order to avoid any contact with foreign economies.
They all adopted communist governments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which groups were excluded from the prosperity many Americans enjoyed after WWII?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: black citizens and women
working and middle classes
returning veterans
suburban families

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways was the containment policy reflected in U.S. involvement in global conflicts such as Korea and Vietnam?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States entered both the Korean and Vietnam Wars to stop the spread of communism.
The United States supported the reunification efforts of communist and non-communist governments.
The United States remained neutral in both conflicts to avoid provoking the Soviets or China.
The containment strategy was to remove communism from countries where it already existed, leading to U.S. involvement in both conflicts.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What fundamental difference caused tension between the United States (U.S.) and Soviet Union (USSR) after World War II?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: economic and political ideologies
religious beliefs
post-war reparations
military alliances

assessment_items.question_content:
**What motivated Stalin to increase Soviet control over Eastern Europe after World War II?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Stalin wanted to create a buffer zone to protect the Soviet Union from future attacks.
Stalin wanted to promote and protect communist regimes in Eastern Europe.
Stalin wanted Eastern European countries to have full independence and self-rule.
Stalin wanted to encourage the spread of capitalism and free-market systems across Europe.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what way did the fall of the Berlin Wall signal the end of the Cold War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It marked the collapse of communist control in Eastern Europe and the end of the East-West divide.
It showed the Soviet Union had won the Cold War and was expanding into Western Europe.
It proved Gorbachev’s policies of perestroika and glasnost were a success and Western Europe was more accepting of communist control.
It showed the strength of NATO and the United States willingness to use direct force against the Soviets.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the United States have a vested interest in preventing communism in the former Belgian Congo?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United States wanted to maintain access to the Congo’s valuable natural resources.
The United States felt empathy for the Congo’s anti-colonial movement.
The United States believed the Congo was a strong American ally.
The United States wanted to support Patrice Lumumba’s leadership.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did British colonial policies deepen social divisions in South Asia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The British encouraged division by pitting ethnic and religious groups against each other.
They encouraged cooperation and social equality.
The British created one state religion and refused to recognize religious distinctions.
Their drawn-out exit strategy united various groups in opposition to their continued colonization.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the lasting impact of U.S. support for Islamic jihadist in Afghanistan during the Cold War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: U.S. support led to the rise of extremist groups and global terrorism.
U.S. support helped Afghanistan stop the spread of communism and become a democratic ally of the West.
U.S. support resulted in Afghanistan’s long-term political and economic stability.
U.S. support strengthened the Soviet Union and prolonged the Cold War.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What best summarizes the impact of U.S. involvement in Latin America, specifically in Cuba and Guatemala, during the Cold War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The U.S. used covert operations to stop communism, creating instability and distrust.
The U.S. supported the democratically elected leaders in Latin America, fostering peace in the region.
American owned companies worked with local farmers to promote economic stability.
The U.S. worked with communist-led countries to weaken Soviet influence in Latin America.

assessment_items.question_content:
>It is logical that the United States should do whatever it is able to do to assist in the return of normal economic health in the world, without which there can be no political stability and no assured peace. Its purpose should be the revival of a working economy in the world so as to permit the emergence of political and social conditions in which free institutions can exist.

>—Secretary of State George C. Marshall, June 1947

**Based on the passage, what was the main goal of the Marshall Plan?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The main goal was to promote stability and support democratic institutions.
The main goal was to form a military alliance to stop the spread of communism.
The main goal was to focus on domestic concerns by isolating the United States from Europe.
The main goal was to provide aid to communist countries in Eastern Europe.
